coredata.dc:title: Near-infrared light triggered photo-therapy, in combination with chemotherapy using magnetofluorescent carbon quantum dots for effective cancer treating
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Ming
Wang, Wentao
Zhou, Ninglin
Yuan, Ping
Su, Yutian
Shao, Maoni
Chi, Cheng
Pan, Feiyan
coredata.dc:description: Magnetofluorescent carbon quantum dots (MCQDs) have attracted significant attention in biomedical studies due to their major role in cancer photothermal therapeutics. We synthesized the FeN@CQDs with intrinsic photoluminescent and magnetic properties with a green, hydrothermal method. These magnetofluorescent FeN@CQDs were conjugated with a folic acid and riboflavin (Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs) as the light-triggered theranostics for simultaneous photothermal therapy (PTT) and photodynamic therapy (PDT). In order to reduce Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs biological toxicity, we used a highly efficient cross-linking reaction to incorporate Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs nanostructures into polymer nanospheres. Doxorubicin, an anticancer drug, was further incorporated into the GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs to form GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX to enable targeted drug delivery. The uptake into cancer cells and the intracellular location of the GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX were observed by confocal laser scanning microscopy. The results of both in vitro and in vivo experiments reveal that the developed can deliver anti-cancer drugs to target cells, release them intracellular upon NIR irradiation, and effectively eliminate tumors through chemo-photo synergistic therapeutic effect.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photothermal therapy
Photodynamic therapy
NIR irradiation
Chemo-photo

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of FeN@CQDs (inset: histograms of particles size distribution of FeN@CQDs and HRTEM image of individual FeN@CQDs). (B) EDS element mapping of C, N, and Fe of the FeN@CQDs. (C) XRD patterns of FeN@CQDs (inset: digital images of the effect of external magnetic stimulation upon FeN@CQDs solution). (D) XPS spectra of FeN@CQDs. (E) Fe 2p spectra of FeN@CQDs. (F) T2 relaxation time of MGQDs from M.R.I. (inset: digital images of FeN@CQDs as contrast agents, as a function of increasing Fe concentration). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) TEM image of GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs nanoparticles. The inserts shows a single GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs nanoparticles. (B) The TGA curves of FA-FeN@CQDs and GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs nanoparticles. (C) FT-IR spectra of FA, FA-FeN@CQDs, Rf, GP, and GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs. (D) Fluorescence spectra of Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs with different excitation wavelengths. (E) Zeta potential of FeN@CQDs, FA-FeN@CQDs, Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs, GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs, DOX and GP-Rf-HA-FeN@CQDs-DOX. The inserts show the FeN@CQDs (a), FA-FeN@CQDs (b), Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs (c), GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs (d), and GP-Rf-HA-FeN@CQDs-DOX solution (e). (F) The changes of fluorescence intensity at the characteristic peak of SOSG (530 nm) as a function of laser irradiation time. The concentration of Rf and GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs is fixed at 1 μM. The mixture solutions were irradiated with a 671 nm laser (10 mW/cm2). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) The absorption of water-dispersible GP-Rf-HA-FeN@CQDs (50 μg/mL). (B) The photothermal heating curves of pure water and GP-Rf-HA-FeN@CQDs with different concentrations under 671-nm laser irradiation at the power density of 2 W/cm2. (C) Temperature IR images of GP-Rf-HA-FeN@CQDs (a) aqueous solution (200 μg/mL) and PBS (b) under 671 nm laser (2 W/cm2) irradiation recorded with an IR camera. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of the GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX obtained at different excitation wavelengths. The inserts show the FeN@CQDs, FA-FeN@CQD, Rf-FA-FeN@CQD, GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQD nanoparticles and GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQD-DOX solution under UV light of 365 nm. (B) DOX release from GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX overtime in buffers at the different pH values (5.0, 6.0 and 7.4) and the NIR irradiation triggered release of DOX from GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQD-DOX at pH values of 5.0. HeLa (C) and HepG2 cells (D) viability at different concentrations of GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX for 24 h at 37 °C with or without irradiation for 3 min with a 671 nm laser (30 mW/cm2). (E) Up-converted photoluminescence spectra of FeN@CQDs. (F) LSCM images of HeLa cells incubated with GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX for 5 h observed under (a) 488 nm, 543 nm (b), the merged images of (a) and (b), and the merged images of (c) and bright field, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
A diagram illustrating stimuli-responsive drug release from GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX (A) and the possible mechanism of cancer phototherapy (B). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) Thermal images of tumor-bearing mice exposed to the 671 nm laser irradiation for 8 min after the injection of GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs (b) and GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX (c). (B) Photographs of mice after various treatments on the 16 days. (C) Representative images of tumors after various treatments on the 16 days. (D) HeLa tumor growth curves after various treatments (n = 5). (E) Mice body weight curves after various treatments (n = 5). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
H&E staining of the tumor, liver, lung, heart, kidney, and spleen after 16 days of treatment with various materials. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Near-infrared light triggered photo-therapy, in combination with chemotherapy using magnetofluorescent carbon quantum dots for effective cancer treating

Jiangsu Engineering Research Center for Biomedical Function Materials

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center for Biological Functional Materials, College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Nanjing Normal University, Nanjing 210023, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Collaborative Innovation Center for Biological Functional Materials
College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Magnetofluorescent carbon quantum dots (MCQDs) have attracted significant attention in biomedical studies due to their major role in cancer photothermal therapeutics. We synthesized the FeN@CQDs with intrinsic photoluminescent and magnetic properties with a green, hydrothermal method. These magnetofluorescent FeN@CQDs were conjugated with a folic acid and riboflavin (Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs) as the light-triggered theranostics for simultaneous photothermal therapy (PTT) and photodynamic therapy (PDT). In order to reduce Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs biological toxicity, we used a highly efficient cross-linking reaction to incorporate Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs nanostructures into polymer nanospheres. Doxorubicin, an anticancer drug, was further incorporated into the GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs to form GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX to enable targeted drug delivery. The uptake into cancer cells and the intracellular location of the GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX were observed by confocal laser scanning microscopy. The results of both in vitro and in vivo experiments reveal that the developed can deliver anti-cancer drugs to target cells, release them intracellular upon NIR irradiation, and effectively eliminate tumors through chemo-photo synergistic therapeutic effect.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid development of science and technology, miniaturization is the goal of equipment and instrument [1,2]. Thus nanomaterials are paid much attention, duo to their special structural characteristics and optical characteristics [3,4]. Quantum dots with excellent emission property, nanometal with surface plasmons resonance (SPR) properties, magnetic nanoparticles with superparamagnetism are three important nanomaterials [5–7]. Compared to traditional fluorescent dyes, quantum dots have many advantages, such as continuously adjustable emission spectrum, good resistance to bleach, good stability and so on [8,9]. Due to the localized surface plasmons, metal nanoparticles can enhance the fluorescence intensity of luminescent materials, which distribute around the metal particles [10]. Metal nanoparticle with special structure can work as light absorbers and produce localized high temperatures to kill tumor cells [11]. Photothermal therapy is a non-interventive, non-complaints care method [12]. Magnetic nanoparticles with superparamagnetism have many applications such as drug delivery magnetic separation and hyperthermia. Recently, increasing attention has been diverted to the fabrication of bifunctional nanostructures consisting two kinds of materials with different properties, such as the nanoparticles possessing magnetic and luminescent property [13,14].

Photodynamic therapy (PDT) is an extraordinary theranostic modality for a number of malignant and nonmalignant diseases. Its principle is photosensitizer selectively accumulates and retention in tissue, under the illumination of special wavelength, tumor tissue can be killed by photochemical or photobiological reaction, thus to achieve the purpose of partly treating tumor [15–17]. At the same time, the heat generated by the penetrating NIR radiation can be envisaged to kill the targeted cancer cells, which is the principle for photothermal therapy (PTT). PTT has been increasingly regarded as a highly selective and minimally invasive approach for cancer treatment [18,19]. However, in the practices of many photosensitizers used in PDT have several deficiencies such as short tissue penetration depth, limited tumor specificity and phototoxicity under sunlight, especially when photosensitizers inevitably distribute in skin and produce 1O2 under sunshine, which may result in serious skin photosensitivity [20–22]. However, the efficiency of the cancer therapy using PTT or PDT alone is limited. Combining methods of treatment is a more promising efficient strategy for eradicating cancer. Therefore, the development of an effective method to produce theranostic nanoparticles (TNPs) as PDT, PTT and chemotherapy for cancer diagnosis and treatment may open a new path for the biomedical uses of magnetic quantum dots (MQDs) [23,24]. For example, photothermal therapy using gold nanostructures combined with chemotherapy or photodynamic therapy appears to be a more aggressive approach compared to any individual method of treatment as described [25,26]. Several in vitro and in vivo studies indicate that some magnetic CQDs are intrinsically cytotoxic because they release free radicals of heavy metals from the CQDs core into the blood stream [27]. Up to now, the reported metal based, carbon-based QDs/PTT can effectively produce high contrast intensity of cell imaging and PTT efficiency [28,29], but they suffer from complex multicomponent system and poor biocompatibility and biodegradability. Consequently, it has become increasingly urgent to address these existing issues for the clinical translation of QDs/PTT. Thus, it is a challenge to develop an economical, simple, biocompatible, and facile method to synthesize nano-theranostics materials with high magnetic and fluorescent property.

The general procedure of PDT involves the systemic, local, or topical administration of a non-toxic drug or dye known as a photosensitizer (PS) followed by selective illumination with appropriate wavelength and power of light [30]. In the presence of oxygen, PS can transfer the absorbed photon energy to surrounding oxygen molecules, generating reactive oxygen species (ROS) including singlet oxygen (SO) or free radicals and consequently causing cell death and tissue destruction [31]. Riboflavin (Rf) is a component of the B2 vitamin complex and a precursor of the redox coenzymes flavin mononucleotide and flavin adenine dinucleotide. Due to its photosensitive property, this vitamin has broad biological applications, such as reducing pathogens and inactivating white blood cells in donated blood products, as well as presenting antitumoral effects [32]. Therefore, Rf enables online imaging of drug for the detection of disease, image-guided drug delivery and treatments, guidance of surgical resection, and monitoring of treatment response [33]. Unfortunately, Rf generally was excited by blue or UV light that shows very limited penetration depth about a few hundred micrometers. For the Rf photosensitisation, one needs to devise means of conversion NIR to UV-blue light at the sub-centimeter depth. With this in mind, Rf was grafted onto the surface of magnetic CQDs to form composite nanoparticles, which is complemented by a depth-resolved structural imaging modality. CQDs with the unique upconversion photoluminescence could serve as an energy donor useful in triggering PDT under NIR light, which offers greatly improved tissue penetration [34].

In this work, we report the preparation of fluorescent FeN@CQDs by a facile, green, and low-cost hydrothermal method using iron crosslinked chitosan complexes (Ch-Fe-CL) as the precursor [35]. To prepare Ch-Fe-CL conjugates, the iron crosslinked chitosan complexes were first synthesized the same as reported in the literature. Our strategy involves the one-pot hydrothermal assisted fabrication of magnetofluorescent FeN@CQDs. This step is followed by functionalizing the FeN@CQDs with a targeting ligand (folic acid, FA) and conjugation of photosensitizer (riboflavin, Rf). In order to reduce FeN@CQDs biological toxicity, we then used a highly efficient cross-linking reaction to incorporate FeN@CQDs nanostructures into polymer nanospheres. Further, chemotherapeutic molecules, doxorubicin (DOX), were effectively loaded through π-π tacking and hydrogen bonding interactions with GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs (Fig. S1). The GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX demonstrated strong NIR absorption and high photothermal conversion efficiency. The release of DOX could be selectively stimulated by NIR-light, enabling intracellular drug accumulation and thereby enhancing the efficiency to kill cancer cell.

Chitosan, folic acid, riboflavin, and genipin was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Doxorubicin (DOX), Hydroxy-2,5-dioxopyrrolidine-3-sulfonicacid sodium salt (Sulfo-NHS, 97%) and 1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC, 99%) was purchased from shanghai Aladdin Company (Shanghai, China). FeCl3·6H2O, sodium acrylate (CH2=CHCOONa), ethylene glycol (EG) and diethylene glycol (DEG) was purchased from Macklin Biochemical Company (Shanghai, China). Double-distilled water (18.2 MΩ) was obtained from Milli-Q system (Millipore, Bedford, MA, USA) and was used in the experiments. Adult whole blood was provided by Jiangsu blood centre. Dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM) were purchased from Thermo Fisher HyClone (USA). Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was obtained from SunShine Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (China). Methyl thiazolyl tetrazolium (MTT) was purchased from Amresco (USA). Hoechst 33258 was purchased from Gibco Co., Ltd. (China). The HeLa, HepG2, and A549 cells were purchased from the Cell Bank of Culture Collection of Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China).

The photoluminescent (PL) spectra were recorded using molecular fluorescence spectrometer (Cary Eclipse, varian, USA). Nexus 670 FTIR type (Nicolet) infrared spectrometer was used to analyze the infrared spectrum of the sample. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed using a D/Max 2500V/PC diffractometer (Rigaku Corporation, Japan). The thermogravimetric (TG) measurements were performed on a Perkin-Elmer TG 7 instrument using a heating rate of 10 °C/min up to 800 °C in nitrogen atmosphere. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were recorded using UV absorption spectrophotometer (Cary-50, varian, USA). The surface composition and element analysis of the samples were recorded using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, EscaLab-250, Thermo, USA). The Malvern ZEN 3600 Zetasizer (Malvern Instruments, UK) was used to study the prepared hydrodynamic size and Zeta potential. MR images were acquired by a 7 T BioSpec 70/30 experimental scanner (70/30 Bruker BioSpin; Ettlingen, Germany). The morphologies of the samples were characterized using the transmission electron microscope (TEM, H-7650, Hitachi, Japan) and scanning electron microscope (SEM, JSM-5610LV, Japan). A Siemens Trio TIM 3 T M.R.I. was used to measure the T2 relaxation times. In vitro bright field and fluorescence images were collected with a confocal laser scanning microscope (TI-E-A1R, Nikon, Japan). To monitor the temperature changes at the tumor site during irradiation, infrared thermal images were recorded with a PTT monitoring system MG33 (Shanghai Magnity Electronics Co. Ltd).

The preparation procedure of the Fe-crosslinked chitosan complex was the same as reported in the literature [35]. Chitosan was dissolved in a FeCl3·6H2O 0.1 M aqueous solution for 4 h. The solid was filtered and washed with acetone to remove an excess of FeCl3·6H2O and finally dried in vacuum. Next, the solid, sodium acrylate (CH2 CHCOONa) and a mixture of ethylene glycol (EG) and diethylene glycol (DEG) was placed in beaker, and stirred for several minutes. Then the solution was transferred into 25 mL of Teflon lined stainless autoclave. The sealed autoclave was heated to 200 °C in an electric oven and kept for additional 10 h. The final product solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (MWCO 1000 Da) for 2 days (changed ultrapure water every 8 h). The solid powders were collected through freeze drying for further characterization and use. The resultant solid products can be easily redispersed into water. N@CQDs were synthesized by a method similar to the preparation of FeN@CQDs, but without FeCl3·6H2O.

The NH2 groups of FA were conjugated to the COOH groups on the surface of FeN@CQD using carbodiimide crosslinker chemistry. Typically, FA was incubated in pH 4.0 of MES buffer solution overnight. To the above HA solution, 0.3 mmol of EDC and equivalent NHS were added at pH 7.0 and the activation of FA was maintained for 4 h. Next, 1 mL of EDC/Sulfo-NHS activated FA was added dropwise into 4 mL of the FeN@CQDs solution [37]. The mixture was stirred in a dark condition for 20 h at room temperature. The obtained FeN@CQDs conjugated with FA is referred to as FA-FeN@CQDs. Further purification of the FA-FeN@CQDs was conducted to remove unbound FA and unreacted chemicals through dialysis tubing (MWCO 500–1000 Da) for one day in the dark. Distilled water (500 mL) was changed at least two times with 4 h intervals.

FA-conjugated FeN@CQDs (FA-FeN@CQDs) were terminated with carboxyl groups. Covalent binding of Rf to the FeN@CQDs-NH2 was performed using a modification of the standard EDC–NHS reaction. Typically, 2 mg of FA-FeN@CQDs was dissolved in 1 mL of double-distilled water (DDW), then 1 molar equivalent of NHS and EDC were then added to the FA-FeN@CQDs solution. The mixture was kept at room temperature for 1 h in the dark for the activation of FA-FeN@CQDs via EDC/SulfoNHS chemistry. Following activation of carboxyl groups, 1 mg of Rf in DMSO were added and the mixed solution was allowed to react at room temperature for 12 h. The resultants were washed five times by distilled water and ethanol alternatively to remove unreacted chemicals by centrifugation at 14000 rpm for 10 min. The products were collected through freeze-drying for further characterization and use.

Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs (1 mg) was dispersed in 1 mL double-distilled water (DDW) to form a homogeneous solution, after which aqueous Genipin (0.5 wt %) was slowly added to the FeN@CQDs solution for crosslinking reaction at 60 °C for 4 h to obtain GP-crosslinked Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs nanoparticles (GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs) [38]. And the solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to remove large particles. Then, powders were obtained by vacuum freeze-drying machine. Finally, the powder were rinsed twice with ethyl acetate then freeze-dried and stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C prior to use.

Specific experimental procedure is as follows: firstly, a small amount of the quinine sulfate (Φst = 0.54) was dissolved in 0.1 M solution of H2SO4, and the FeN@CQDs, N@CQDs, and GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs were dissolved in deionized water. Then, the absorbance values of FeN@CQDs, N@CQDs, and GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs and quinine sulfate were measured at 360 nm. Ensuring both of the absorbance values were less than 0.05. In addition, the integral fluorescence intensity of FeN@CQDs, N@CQDs, and GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs and quinine sulfate were obtained by measuring both of PL emission spectra at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Finally, the quantum yield of FeN@CQDs, N@CQDs, and GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs was calculated using following equation:
Φ =
Φ
st
(
K x
/
K
st
)
(
η x
/
η
st
)
2
where Φ is the quantum yield, K is the slope determined by the curve, and ηx/ηst = 1. The subscript “st” and “x” denote the standard and samples, respectively [36].

DOX was loaded into the GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs nanoparticles by dialysis method as described in previous studies [38]. To prepare the blank GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs solution, GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs (0.2 mM) were dissolved in ultrapure water followed by treatment of ultrasonicator at room temperature. DOX (0.1 mg/mL) was added into the yellow GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs solution and stirred for 12 h in a dark place. Following the mixed solution was subject to dialysis (molecular weight cutoff of 1000 Da) against deionized water for 48 h. The quantitation of DOX calculated by the absorbance of DOX at 485 nm.
DOX
loading
ratio =
( weight
of
drug
in
nanocarrier / weight
of
nanocarrier
taken )
× 100 %

To study the drug release, a 3 mL solution of GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQD-DOX was placed in a centrifuge tube in PBS with different pH values (7.4, 6.0 or 5.0) under 37 °C water bath. 0.5 mL of solution was centrifuged, and the amount of released DOX in the supernatant was determined using UV–vis spectrometry. At designed time intervals, the samples were irradiated by the 671 nm laser with a power intensity of 2 W/cm2 for 5 min [39].
DOX release efficiency=(weight of DOX released from GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQD-DOX/weight of DOX in GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQD-DOX) × 100%.

Aqueous–dispersion GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs (1.0 mL) with different concentrations (0 mg/mL to 0.2 mg/mL) were introduced in a quartz cuvette and irradiated with 671 nm laser at a power density of 2 W/cm2 for 8 min to measure the photothermal conversion performance of GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs. Pure water was used as a negative control. A thermocouple probe with an accuracy of 0.1 °C was inserted into the aqueous solution of GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs in a position perpendicular to the light path. Temperature was recorded every 10 s by a digital thermometer with a thermocouple probe [40].

Two plates were set up as two dark control groups and two laser exposed groups for the HeLa and HepG2 assay and these plates were seeded. The cells of experimental groups were then rinsed again with PBS and incubated with different concentrations of GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs and GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX for 24 h at 37 °C in the dark before being illuminated using a 671 nm laser with energy density of 30 mW/cm2 for 3 min. After illumination, cells were incubated for 48 h in a 5% CO2, 95% air humidified incubator at 37 °C. Dark control group is kept identical to that laser exposed group except for illumination [40].

To study mammalian cell imaging, the cellular uptake of DOX and GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX by HeLa cells was investigated using a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM). The cells were seed at a density of 1 × 105 cells/well onto 35-mm Petri dish and incubated at 37 °C for 12 h. Then DOX and GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX solution was diluted to a final concentration of 300 μg/mL by liquid medium, then cells were incubated with DOX and GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX for 3 h at 37 °C. The cells monolayer was washed three times with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, PH = 7.4) and fixed on a solid with 3% paraformaldehyde for 30 min. Cellular uptake and DOX delivery were detected using a confocal laser scanning microscope [41].

All animal operations were in accordance with institutional animal use and care regulations, Nanjing normal University. Cancer cells were subcutaneously inoculated into female nude mice (25 g) to form in vivo tumors. To form a malignant liver tumor model, 1 × 106 human liver cancer cells, HeLa, collected in 100 μL DMEM, were implanted subcutaneously in the left and right axillaries of nude mice. The mice were used for photodynamic therapy when the tumor volume reached about 100–120 mm3.

After the tumor volume reached about 100 mm3 (on the 10th day after inoculation), according to the tumor volume, a total of 25 nude mice were averagely divided into 5 groups, namely an average of 5 mice in each group: saline-treatment only; DOX-treatment only; GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX-treatment only; GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-treatment and irradiation; GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX-treatment and irradiation. For convention treatment, Mice in control group received tail vein injections of PBS solution (100 μL, pH 7.4). Mice in experimental groups received DOX (1 mg/mL) and GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX (1 mg/mL) by tail vein injection. For PDT and PTT treatment, GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs (1 mg/mL) and GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX solution (1 mg/mL) were delivered via tail vein injection, and after 8 h circulation, a 1.5 cm diameter area encompassing the tumor was irradiated for 8 min using a 671 nm laser (2 W/cm2). Tumor volume (V = 1/2 ab2 mm3) was determined by measuring tumor long diameter (a mm) and short diameter (b mm) using a digital caliper.

The tissues (heart, liver, spleen, kidneys, and lung) were harvested and fixed in 10% formalin solution. The histopathological tests were performed according to standard laboratory procedures. Tissue samples were numbered and given blind to a pathologist for conventional processing and analysis. The tissue samples were embedded in paraffin blocks, sectioned into 5 μm slices, and mounted onto the glass slides. After H&E staining, the sections were observed, and photos were taken using an optical microscope (liver, heart, spleen, lung, kidney, and tumor).

To prepare FeN@CQDs conjugates (Fig. S1), the iron crosslinked chitosan complexes were first synthesized the same as reported in the literature (Ch-Fe-CL). Then, magnetofluorescent FeN@CQDs were produced in a one-pot reaction by hydrothermal synthesis method. To render the carriers with the function of targeting, FeN@CQDs were modified with FA molecules. As we known, photodynamic therapy (PDT) is an extraordinary theranostic modality for a number of malignant and nonmalignant diseases. In addition, the biocompatibility of FeN@CQDs embedded inside polymer spheres may be better than that of the FeN@CQDs-coated polymer spheres because the heavy metal material is protected by the polymer spheres in the former structure [42]. Herein, we strategically designed and prepared novel multifunctional and low toxicity GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQD nanoparticles as the light-triggered theranostics for enhanced PDT. Finally, DOX was successfully loaded onto the FeN@CQD via formation of metal-DOX complex and covalent interactions.

The morphology and size distribution of the synthesized FeN@CQDs and GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQD were investigated with TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the FeN@CQDs were well-dispersed and approximately spherical in shape. A histogram in the inset of Fig. 1A shows the size distribution taken from over 150 nanoparticles and has an average size of 6.2 nm. DLS measurements showed that the average diameter of FeN@CQDs and was 5.8 nm (Fig. S2). This value is also consistent with the size determined from TEM images because DLS measures the overall hydrodynamic size of particles. High-resolution TEM image of FeN@CQDs revealed clear lattice fringes with an interplanar distance of 0.29 nm, which corresponds to the (220) lattice spacing of cubic Fe3O4, as marked in red circles. (inset of Fig. 1A). The as-synthesized N@CQDs were quasi-spherical with well-defined lattice fringes identical to graphitic nature in Fig. S3A. To obtain the structural information about FeN@CQDs, X-ray diffraction (XRD) characterization was conducted (Fig. 1C). N@CQDs are characteristic of rGO ((002) peak at 2θ = 23.5). Fe3O4 is crystalline, with a (220) peak at 2 h = 30.1, a strong (311) peak at 2θ = 35.2, a (400) peak at 2θ = 43.1, a (422) peak at 2θ = 57.2, and a (511) peak at 2θ = 62.8. The FeN@CQDs curve shows a change in structure from both N@CQDs and Fe3O4. Six diffraction lines were observed in the representative XRD pattern of FeN@CQDs at 2θ = 30.1, 35.6, 43.3, 57.5, 63.1, and 74.7. These diffraction lines can be assigned to the (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), and (533) reflections, respectively. As indicated in the inset of Fig. 1C, the FeN@CQDs could be separated from the aqueous dispersion in a short time by an applied magnetic field.

The composition of FeN@CQDs was identified by XPS measurement. Fig. 1D displayed the survey X-ray photoelectron spectral (XPS) scan of FeN@CQDs, revealing that FeN@CQDs are mainly composed of carbon, phosphorus, nitrogen, and oxygen with the binding energy peaks at 532.8, 401.3, 285.6, and 731.4 eV corresponding to O 1s, N 1s, C 1s, and Fe 2p, respectively. The results in the inset of Fig. 1D revealed the presence of C 62.95, N 5.00, O 25.92, and Fe (calculated) 1.14 wt %. In the Fe 2p spectrum (Fig. 1E), the peaks for Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2 were observed at 711.89 and 725.12 eV, which is also indicative of the formation of a Fe3O4 phase in the FeN@CQDs [43]. The distributions of the C, N, and Fe atoms on the FeN@CQDs were also consistent with the element determined from quantitative energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). As shown in Fig. 1B, the C, N, and Fe atoms were homogeneously distributed over the surfaces of the FeN@CQDs. The high-resolution C 1s spectrum in Fig. S3B demonstrates five peaks at 284.29, 284.87, 285.52, 286.1, and 287.32 eV, which are associated with C 1s states in CC, CC, COH/COC, CN, and CO, respectively. Fig. S3C displays the N 1s spectrum which can be deconvoluted into four peaks at 397.85, 398.35, 399.0, and 399.83, 400.6, and 401.4 eV representing N 1s states in CNC, N(C)3 functionalities, pyridinic-like N, pyrrolic-like N, NH, and NO, respectively. The N 1s peak of the chitosan centered at 399.3 eV was associated with aromatic N and nitrogen in an NH2 state, whereas the N 1s peak of FeN@CQDs at 400.1 eV was assigned to NH2 and N at the surface [44]. The O 1s spectrum of the FeN@CQDs in Fig. S3D is deconvoluted into four peaks at 531.8, 532.5, 533.3, and 530.0 eV, which are contributed to OC, ON, OC, and COC, respectively.

A further investigation of the structure of the FeN@CQDs and the structural change during autoclaving was performed through FTIR spectroscopy of Ch, Ch-Fe-CL and FeN@CQDs (Fig. S4A). Ch has CO bonds at 1043 cm−1, OH/NH bonds at 3423 cm−1, CN bonds at 1173 cm−1. For the Ch-Fe-CL, the synthesis procedure of Fe3+ on the Ch has led to the shift and exhibited increased peak intensity in the region 3370 cm−1-3250 cm−1 and 1650 cm−1-1550 cm−1. For example, the absorption bands of FeN@CQDs revealed a shift to higher absorption around 3443 cm−1 (OH/NH). The FeO peak has been maintained and is at 621 cm−1. The FTIR spectra of FeN@CQDs showed bands near 3431 and 1631 cm−1, which belong to the stretching vibrations of OH/NH, CC/CN, and 1074 and 617 cm−1 peaks attributed to CFe and FeO bonds confirming the coordination effect of Fe ions [45].

The optical properties were measured by UV–vis and photoluminescence (PL) spectra. The UV–vis spectra of FeN@CQDs showed two characteristic absorption peaks around 248 and 328 nm (Fig. S6A). The peaks are probably due to (π-π∗) transition of CC at the aromatic region and (n-π*) transition of the heteroatom (N) doped surface state region, respectively [46]. As shown in Fig. S4, exploring the optical properties of the as-synthesized FeN@CQDs were investigated. Fig. S4B were the PL spectra of FeN@CQDs, which can be seen that the excitation (λex) and emission (λem) peaks are located at 410 and 470 nm. The photograph of FeN@CQDs dispersion under 365 nm UV light exhibits different blue emission as shown in the inset of Fig. S4B. In quest of further exploring the optical properties of the as-synthesized FeN@CQDs the λex-dependent PL behavior, which is mutual in fluorescent carbon materials was investigated and displayed in Fig. S4C. With increasing excitation wavelength from 280 to 440 nm, the emission peak shifts from 420 to 510 nm. Thus, the λex-dependent PL behavior is useful for multicolor in vitro and in vivo biological imaging applications (Fig. S4E). Furthermore, the fluorescence quantum yield (Φs) of the as-prepared N@CQDs and FeN@CQDs was calculated to be 13.2% and 11.3% using quinine sulfate as a reference [47]. Meanwhile, the PL spectrum clearly shows that the emission intensity was not any significant quenched between N@CQDs and FeN@CQDs (Fig. S4D).

The magnetic properties of FeN@CQDs were investigated through the use of the T2 relaxation time of MGQDs from M.R.I. Fig. 6B was the T2 relaxation time of FeN@CQDs from M.R.I., with the slope of the relaxation time determined as 3.82 mM/s. The T2 relaxation time curves showed an apparent enhancement of relaxation with the concentration of Fe increased. In vitro MR images showed excellent MR performance of FeN@CQDs. The inset of Fig. 1F showed digital images of FeN@CQDs as contrast agents as a function of increasing Fe concentration. The results of the PL and magnetic properties tests confirm that the FeN@CQDs can potentially be used for dual-modality fluorescent and M.R.I. applications.

The actual size and shape of GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs nanoparticles were determined by the TEM and SEM analyses. Interestingly, there are dark particles wrapped in the nanosphere as shown in the magnified image of single GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs nanoparticles (Fig. 2 A,a,b,c), suggesting that Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs are wrapped after chemical crosslinking reaction. The images of TEM revealed that the GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs nanoparticles were spherical in shape with an average diameter of 120 ± 10 nm, and had good dispersity in water, suggesting that the GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs nanoparticles is well within the preferred range of the nanoparticles used for drug-delivery [48]. It is confirmed from the SEM image that the particles are uniform in shape and their size is about 125 ± 10 nm (Fig. S4B). In support of the TEM and SEM results, the dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis further indicated that the GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs nanoparticles had an average diameter of 125 ± 20 nm (inset of Fig. S5I). TEM was employed to observe GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs nanoparticles morphology at different stages. Fig. S5 shows the TEM images of the samples with different chemical crosslinking time. It can be seen that the micelle-like structure forms at 1 h (Fig. S5E), suggesting that the assembly between GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs nanosheet is achieved by reaction between GP and Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs. At 2 h (Fig. S5F), GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs nanosheet assemblies tend to become lamella, with a size of 500 ± 50 nm. When crosslinking time extends to 3 h (Fig. S5G), the spherical assemblies become smaller and the swollen shell decreases markedly with increased crosslinking degree. At the crosslinking time of 4 h (Fig. S5H), the uniformly dispersed GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs nanoparticles with diameters of 110 ± 10 nm are finally fabricated with rough surfaces due to the crosslinking of Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs, which are favorable for high drug-loading. For biomedical applications, good dispersion of nanomaterials in a physiological medium is a prime merit.

A key criterion for the use of any nanoparticles in biomedical applications is the ability to maintain the size of the nanoparticles or their aggregates below 4 μm, which is the smallest diameter of human blood capillaries, and thus to prevent a blockage of the blood vessels or from being eliminated by the body's reticuloendothelial system. We investigated the physical stabilities of GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs nanoparticles in different media, GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs nanoparticles were dispersed very well in different solutions without obviously macroscopic aggregates (Fig. S5A). The thermostability of GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQD nanoparticles was investigated by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) in a nitrogen (N2) atmosphere, as displayed in Fig. 2B. Compared with FeN@CQDs, an additional weight loss of GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs nanoparticles was about 23%, which could be ascribed to the formation of GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs nanoparticles.

FA and Rf were conjugated to the surface of FeN@CQDs, using a traditional EDC/Sulfo-NHS chemical reaction. UV–vis absorption, PL, and zeta potential analysis were employed to confirm the FA and Rf molecules were conjugated on the surface of the FeN@CQDs. The FTIR spectra of FA indicated strong COC (1070 cm−1) vibrations due to epoxy ether groups. This peak was also observed in the FTIR spectra of FA-FeN@CQDs (Fig. 2C). Additionally, the spectra of Rf showed the characteristic absorption peak at 1735 and 1397 cm−1, which was also observed in the FTIR spectra of GP-Rf-HA-FeN@CQDs nanoparticles. The result further indicated that Rf was successfully conjugated to FeN@CQDs. Similarly, the FT-IR spectrum of GP-Rf-HA-FeN@CQDs nanoparticles is similar to that of Rf, FA-FeN@CQDs and GP, which suggests that GP was successfully cross-linked with the Rf-HA-FeN@CQDs. Fig. S6A showed a UV–vis absorbance spectra change of pegylation of FeN@CQDs at 340 nm. However, the absorption of FA-FeN@CQD showed a blue shift to 258 nm, indicating successful conjugation of the FA ligand to FeN@CQDs, due to formation of an amide bond linkage between FA and FeN@CQDs. Rf conjugating to the surface of FeN@CQDs was evidenced by eye due to the yellow color of the Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs suspension. Additionally, a peak of Rf appeared at around 440 nm in the UV–vis spectrum of the Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs. The result demonstrated that Rf molecules have been successfully conjugated to the surface of FeN@CQDs. As shown in the UV–vis absorbance spectra of GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs nanoparticles, the same two Rf peaks were observed for GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs nanoparticles. Since FeN@CQDs and Rf have different fluorescent properties when excited at different wavelengths, fluorescence spectroscopy is a great method to characterize the conjugates [49]. As seen in Fig. 2D, the fluorescence of the GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs nanoparticles shows the same excitation peaks two reactants display when they are tested independently (Fig. S6B for Rf), this data confirms that the conjugate product contains the FeN@CQDs and Rf. The fluorescence of the conjugate displays the typical excitation wavelength dependent shifting property of the FeN@CQDs, which has been demonstrated before in previous publications. Furthermore, the fluorescence quantum yield (Φs) of the as prepared GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs was calculated to be 9.2%. As shown in Fig. 2E and Fig. S7, the zeta potential value for FeN@CQDs at pH 7.4 was −5.5 mV. Further, surface conjugation of FA molecules to the FA-FeN@CQDs led to an increased in the negative zeta potential, due to increased COO− groups on the surface of the FeN@CQDs. However, the zeta potential value for Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs has increased, mostly due to the increased OH− groups on the surface of the Rf-HA-FeN@CQDs. After cross-linking with GP, the zeta potential of GP-Rf-HA-FeN@CQDs nanoparticles turns into −15.7 mV. The samples all bore negative charges, which may be responsible for the high water dispersity and solubility of the as-prepared particles (inset of Fig. 2E) [46].

The generation of ROS by GP-Rf-HA-FeN@CQDs was detected chemically using the singlet oxygen sensor green (SOSG) as a detector. For SOSG detector, intramolecular electron transfer (ET) quenches the fluorescence from the light-emitting chromophore before the reaction with ROS. After reaction with ROS, with the formation of the endoperoxide, the electron transfer is prohibited, and fluorescence is recovered [28]. Fig. 2F shows the increase in fluorescence intensity at the special peak of SOSG (530 nm) as a function of laser irradiation Time. For SOSG alone with laser irradiation group, the fluorescence intensity is a constant. For pure FeN@CQDs with laser irradiation group, the fluorescence intensity is also a constant, which indicates that pure FeN@CQDs alone cannot generate ROS. Compared with Rf with laser irradiation group, the increase of SOSG's fluorescence intensity is lower than the increase in GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs with laser irradiation group, which suggests that the FeN@CQDs can enhance the efficiency of ROS production of Rf on FeN@CQDs. Meanwhile, the results indicate that the ROS can be produced by Rf after conjugation with FA-FeN@CQDs. Therefore, the GP-Rf-HA-FeN@CQDs could be used for in vivo tumor PDT treatment.

Fig. 3 A showed the UV–vis spectrum of GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs, with broad absorption from 400 to 750 nm. Their absorption in the NIR region from 650 to 750 nm makes the as-prepared GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs highly promising for PTT. To confirm the photothermal effect, temperature changes of the GP-Rf-HA-FeN@CQDs nanoparticles dispersion in PBS were measured upon irradiation using an 671 nm laser (the efficient NIR absorption features between 650 and 800 nm absorption wavelengths). As expected, when irradiated by an 671 nm NIR laser (2 W/cm2), the GP-Rf-HA-FeN@CQDs-DOX solution exhibited an obvious concentration dependent heating effect (Fig. 3B). Under continuous irradiation of 671 nm laser (2 W/cm2), the temperature elevation of the aqueous dispersions containing GP-Rf-HA-FeN@CQDs (200 μg/mL) was measured (Fig. 3C). The control experiment demonstrates that the temperature of pure water (without GP-Rf-HA-FeN@CQDs) only increased by less than 5.8 °C from room temperature. However, with the addition of GP-Rf-HA-FeN@CQDs-DOX (20, 50, 100, and 200 μg/mL), the temperature of the aqueous dispersions increased by 11.8–27.6 °C after a 8 min irradiation (Fig. S8A). These results indicated that GP-Rf-HA-FeN@CQDs can rapidly and efficiently convert laser energy into heat via efficient photoabsorption at 671 nm.

DOX was selected as a model drug to investigate the feasibility of GP-Rf-HA-FeN@CQDs-DOX as a drug delivery carrier. We speculated that DOX was probably conjugated with GP-Rf-HA-FeN@CQDs-DOX through physicochemical interactions, including (π-π) stacking, electrostatic, and amide interactions. Fig. S6A displayed UV–vis absorbance spectra of GP-Rf-HA-FeN@CQDs-DOX and free DOX. The characteristic absorbance peak at 490 nm indicated that DOX was loaded to GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQD nanoparticles successfully. After conjugation with DOX, the GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX exhibits a strong absorption between 550 and 750 nm (Fig. S6A,B). The strong absorption of GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX in the NIR region makes the as-prepared GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX highly promising for PTT [50,51]. The surface zeta potential of FA-FeN@CQD changed from −15.7 to −2.8 mV after DOX conjugation (Fig. 2E and Fig. S7). It suggested that the negative charge (COO−) of the FA-FeN@CQDs conjugated with a positive charge (NH2) on DOX, which may be due to electrostatic interaction. Moreover, GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX is also confirmed to have the anticancer doxorubicin through fluorescence, since the load exhibits its expected doxorubicin emission at around 560 and 590 nm (Fig. S6C for DOX alone) [49]. These emission patterns from the fluorescence spectra prove that the DOX were conjugated to GP-Rf-HA-FeN@CQDs nanoparticles (Fig. 4 A). Also, the loading efficiency of DOX on GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs nanoparticles was calculated by measuring the concentration of unbound drug according to the characteristic absorption of free DOX in the UV–vis spectra (Fig. S8B). The DOX loading capacity increased with increasing amounts of added DOX (Fig. S8C).

To test the drug release profile of GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX, diverse stimuli including pH and NIR light was tested. At pH 5.0, about 60% of DOX was released (Fig. 4B and S8F), while only ca. 22% was released at pH 7.4 after 72 h (Fig. 4B and S8D), indicating that DOX can be released at a much faster and higher rate from GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX in more acidic than neutral medium. And the amount of cumulative released DOX was NIR-light irradiation enhancement. The increased drug release upon NIR-light irradiation could be attributed to the increased temperature due to the effective photothermal heating property of GP-Rf-HA-FeN@CQDs nanoparticles, which may result in the destruction of intermolecular forces and hydrogen bonding [39]. Therefore, the DOX release was reinforced. In Fig. S8G, the UV–vis absorbance intensity exhibited a time-dependent enhancement after the NIR irradiation, which triggered release DOX from GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX at pH values of 5.0. The releasing efficiency of DOX on GP-Rf-HA-FeN@CQDs-DOX was calculated by the UV–vis spectra (Fig. S6A). Consequently, GP-Rf-HA-FeN@CQDs-DOX could release the DOX molecule under exogenous NIR irradiation, and the endogenous lower pH in endolysosome, which would enhance antitumor efficacy and minimize the side effect of the drug.

The in vitro cytotoxicity of free DOX, GP-Rf-HA-FeN@CQDs, and GP-Rf-HA-FeN@CQDs-DOX in HeLa and HepG2 cells was measured in an MTT assay to examine the potential GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX intracellular therapeutic effect. Cell viability was normalized to control cells (nonirradiated). As shown in Fig. 4C and D, exposure of tumor cells to GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX for 24 h followed by laser irradiation induced a concentration-dependent cytotoxicity to HeLa and HepG2 cells, which was significantly different from nonirradiated controls, DOX alone controls, and GP-Rf-HA-FeN@CQDs laser irradiation controls. With the increase of drug concentration, the cell viability gradually decreased. Interestingly, HeLa cells exhibited higher cytotoxicity compared to HepG2 cells. This could be explained by the presence of more positive folate receptors on the surface of HeLa cells. The excellent biocompatibility and targeting ability towards cancer cells enabled us to conclude that the GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX have a high potential for drug loading, image guided target delivery of a drug and photodynamic therapy that has the potential to induce cancer cell death. Interestingly, the chemotherapy, PDT, and PTT effect of GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX is stronger than the chemotherapy effect of GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX alone and the PDT and PTT effect of GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs, which suggests that GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX is advantageous over the conventional formulation, which is attributed to the enhanced efficiency of photoproducts of riboflavin, and the released capacity of DOX or the light-thermal conversion of GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs. The mechanism may relate with FeN@CQDs that were capable to convert deeply-penetrating near-infrared light into UV-blue light by resonant energy transfer processes to photosensitise Rf using the moderate intensity and doses of NIR light acceptable for PDT procedures. Rf are characterized by a broad UV-blue absorption band with two peaks at 375 and 450 nm, corresponding to the singlet S2 and S1 excited states, respectively. Rf in the long-lived T1 state is capable to non-radiatively (collisionally) drive environmental oxygen from its triplet ground state 3O2 to the chemically reactive singlet excited state 1O2 [52]. The mechanism may relate with 1O2 initiated damage of DNA, resulting in multi-gene expressions variation and oxidized incising and shorten telomere (Fig. 5 B). To further verify the photoactivation of fluorophores by FeN@CQDs, photoluminescence (PL) of the as-prepared FeN@CQDs was studied using NIR excitation wavelengths. Fig. 4E showed the upconverted PL spectra (200–800 nm) of FeN@CQDs with excitations at 500, 600, 650, 700, 800, and 900 nm (NIR light region). We found that the emissions are located at visible light wavelengths of 350–600 nm. That is to say, FeN@CQDs have obvious up-converted PL behavior. FeN@CQDs absorb NIR light and then emit visible light (350–530 nm) as a result of up-conversion, which can in turn excite a visible light used for photoactivation of Rf at the sub-centimeter depth. Among these, two UV-blue photoluminescence doublet bands (Fig. 5B) fall squarely into the Rf absorption band, relying on resonance energy transfer processes (RET) of the energy transfer donor-acceptor pair Rf-FeN@CQDs [51].

Another important property of drug delivery systems is ready internalization by cells, which was studied by laser scanning confocal microscopy (LSCM). According to the λex-dependent PL behavior of GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs nanoparticles, the cells became brightly illuminated with green images when they were excited with blue laser pulses. After 5 h incubation, only the cell membrane and cytoplasm exhibited green emission, while the cell nuclei were not infiltrated significantly, as shown in Fig. 4F,a. However, bright red fluorescence from DOX was observed in cell nuclei, indicating that DOX was almost completely released from GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs nanoparticles and entered cell nuclei (Fig. 4F,c and d), which is in complete agreement with free DOX intracellular imaging shown in Fig. S9 and other reports [47]. GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX exhibit excellent photostability, suggesting that it can be serve as a cellular imaging agent. As illustrated in Fig. 5, the cellular uptake and intracellular trafficking mechanisms of GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX can be described as follows: First, GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX form vesicles and enter the cells by pinocytosis. Second, the GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX vesicles transport into the lysosomes. Third, under the acidic environment of lysosomes and endosomes, DOX is released and then enters and accumulates in the nuclei.

To verify the in vivo photothermal conversion effect generated by the GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX, we used an IR thermal mapping apparatus to record the change in temperature in the tumor area (Fig. 6 A). As expected, the temperature of tumor site treated with GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs and GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX rapidly increased and maintained at 50.2 °C during laser irradiation. This temperature is sufficient to cause irreversible damage to cancer cells. In comparison, the maximum temperature of the tumor surface in the laser-only group was only approximately 39.2 °C under the same irradiation condition. The nanocomposites to convert light energy into heat energy and their potential usefulness as an in vivo PTT agent.

The real photographs of tumor-bearing nude mice were recorded in different groups, respectively (Fig. 6B). At 21 days, three mice were sacrificed and tumors were excised (Fig. 6C). The tumor sizes were then measured every four day for 16 days (Fig. 6D). Compared to treatment with free DOX, GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX without laser irradiation showed improved therapeutic effect, probably owing to the enhanced tumor uptake by the active targeting and the enhanced permeability retention (EPR) effect. Although enhanced inhibition of tumor growth was observed with injection of only GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX or GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs with NIR laser irradiation, the tumor growth was only partially delayed, indicating the poor efficacy in inhibiting tumor growth. In marked contrast, the tumor in the combination therapy group (GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX+laser irradiation) was completely eradicated. Moreover, the in vivo toxicity of the GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX was also roughly estimated by monitoring the change in the weight of the mice during the study period, and no obvious side effects were revealed (Fig. 6E). The organ index is defined as the ratio of the organ wet weight to the body weight (g/g). The increase of the organ index means organ congestion, swelling, proliferation, or function is strengthening while the decrease usually means that the organ is shrunken or the function is weakened. Typical measurement data are shown in Fig. S10. There is no significant difference in the spleen, liver heart, lung, and kidney indices of the control and experimental groups, showing that the tumor in the combination therapy group (GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX+laser irradiation) never cause obvious toxicity and side effects on the spleen, liver, heart, lung, and kidney.

In order to further understand the combination therapy effects after various treatments down to the cellular level, hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining was introduced to study the morphology and apoptosis of tumor cells after 2 days post-treatment (Fig. 7 ). The results indicated that cells in the PBS groups mainly retained their normal morphology. There is little cell necrosis on the examined tumor sections of the GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX, DOX, GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs with the laser irradiation groups. In contrast, a large amount of dead cells were observed in the tumor tissue of the GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX with the laser treatment group. No pathological changes were observed in the liver, spleen, kidney, lung, and heart for the mice in GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX with the laser treatment group as compared to those in the control groups. Taken together, the in vivo combination therapy results indicated that the GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX with the laser treatment induced apparent tumor inhibition but no obvious damage to major organs in tumor-bearing mice, revealing a potential platform for treatment of malignant cancers.

In this study, the GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX was developed as NIR adsorbed theranostic agents to realize the combination of chemotherapy, photodynamic therapy, and photothermal therapy of tumor both in vitro and in vivo. The GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX showed NIR absorbance, good photothermal performance, targeting accumulation at tumor sites, as well as excellent biocompatibility. The FeN@CQDs were capable to efficiently convert deeply-penetrating near-infrared light into UV-blue light by resonant energy transfer processes to photosensitise Rf using the moderate intensity and doses of NIR light acceptable for PDT procedures. The loaded DOX could be triggered by external NIR light and low intracellular pH value. Both in vitro and in vivo results demonstrated that GP-Rf-FA-FeN@CQDs-DOX had the ability to achieve synergistic therapeutic effect, and further to effectively eradicate tumor without regrowth. It would have great potential for the clinical applications in treating patients with gastric cancer or other tumors in the near future.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was supported by the Jiangsu six category outstanding talent (2012-NY-031), Jiangsu province science and technology support plan (BE2014327, BE2015367), the Jiangsu Agriculture Science and Technology Innovation Fund CX (15) 1016, JHB05-21 and Nanjing-321, the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD 2016–2017).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2017.03.085.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hand-held miniaturized cardiac ultrasound instruments for rapid and effective bedside diagnosis and patient screening
The interface between forensic science and technology: how technology could cause a paradigm shift in the role of forensic institutes in the criminal justice system
One-pot microwave synthesis of water-dispersible, high fluorescence silicon nanoparticles and their imaging applications in vitro and in vivo
Synthesis and optical properties of SnO2 structures with different morphologies via hydrothermal method
The distribution and degradation of radiolabeled superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles and quantum dots in mice
Surface modified multifunctional ZnFe2O4 nanoparticles for hydrophobic and hydrophilic anti-cancer drug molecule loading
Magnetic/fluorescent barcodes based on cadmium-free near-infrared-emitting quantum dots for multiplexed detection
Polyamine-functionalized carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for selective and sensitive detection of copper ions
Carbon quantum dots and their applications
Self-assembled gold nanoclusters for bright fluorescence imaging and enhanced drug delivery
Near-infrared light-responsive nanomaterials for cancer theranostics
Dopamine-melanin colloidal nanospheres: an efficient near-infrared photothermal therapeutic agent for in vivo cancer therapy
Designed synthesis of uniformly sized iron oxide nanoparticles for efficient magnetic resonance imaging contrast agents
In vivo photodynamic therapy using upconversion nanoparticles as remote-controlled nanotransducers

Photothermal therapy: cancer cell internalization of gold nanostars impacts their photothermal efficiency in vitro and in vivo: toward a plasmonic thermal fingerprint in tumoral environment (Adv. Healthcare mater. 9/2016)
Multimodal imaging guided photothermal therapy using functionalized graphene nanosheets anchored with magnetic nanoparticles
Tissue oxygen monitoring by photoacoustic lifetime imaging (PALI) and its application to image-guided photodynamic therapy (PDT)
Photodynamic therapy for hilar bile duct cancer: clinical evidence for improved tumoricidal tissue penetration by temoporfin
Engineering of multifunctional nano-micelles for combined photothermal and photodynamic therapy under the guidance of multimodal imaging
Near-infrared light-mediated nanoplatforms for cancer thermo-chemotherapy and optical imaging
Ablation of hypoxic tumors with dose-equivalent photothermal, but not photodynamic, therapy using a nanostructured porphyrin assembly
Quantum dot nano thermometers reveal heterogeneous local thermogenesis in living cells
Rapid fabrication of carbon quantum dots as multifunctional nanovehicles for dual-modal targeted imaging and chemotherapy
Upconversion nanoparticles and their composite nanostructures for biomedical imaging and cancer therapy
Nanomaterials formulations for photothermal and photodynamic therapy of cancer
Photodynamic efficiency: from molecular photochemistry to cell death
Main-chain optically active riboflavin polymer for asymmetric catalysis and its vapochromic behavior
Endogenous UVA-photosensitizers: mediators of skin photodamage and novel targets for skin photoprotection
Recent advances in design and fabrication of upconversion nanoparticles and their safe theranostic applications
